continents.name: Europe
continents.summary: Mountain ranges stacked across a continent — the Pyrenees, the Alps, the far north.

countries.name: Spain
countries.tagline: From the Pyrenees to the Picos
countries.summary: Spain runs from the green, Atlantic-washed north to the dry sierras of the south, with the Pyrenees a 400 km mountain wall along the French border.

countries.name: France
countries.tagline: The north flank of the Pyrenees
countries.summary: France meets the Pyrenees along their wetter, greener northern flank — the Pays Basque, the high Béarn and Bigorre, and the long descent through the Ariège to the Mediterranean.

ranges.name: Pyrenees
ranges.tagline: Atlantic to Mediterranean, Spain to France
ranges.summary: A 430 km wall between France and Spain, from the green Atlantic west to the dry Mediterranean east — walked end to end by the GR11 on the Spanish side, the GR10 on the French, and the high HRP between them.

highlights.title: Cabo Higuer: the Atlantic start
highlights.body: The GR11 begins at Cabo Higuer on the Gipuzkoan coast with the Cantabrian Sea at your feet, climbing inland past Hondarribia and San Marcial's chapel before crossing into Navarre at Bera.

highlights.title: The Irati Forest and Irabia reservoir
highlights.body: East of Burguete the trail threads the western edge of the Selva de Irati, one of Europe's largest beech-fir forests, crossing the river Irati on a medieval bridge near Orbara and passing close to the Irabia reservoir.

highlights.title: Sanctuary of Muskilda above Ochagavía
highlights.body: The descent into Ochagavía/Otsagabia, one of Navarre's prettiest mountain villages, passes the hilltop Sanctuary of Muskilda (1,020m) through the Arburua beech wood off the Abodi ridge.

highlights.title: Elizondo and the Baztan valley
highlights.body: The red-stone capital of the Baztan valley, Elizondo opens onto one of the wildest, best-preserved corners of the Navarran mountains, with dense beech forests climbing toward the Urkiaga pass.

highlights.title: Aguas Tuertas valley
highlights.body: One of the most beautiful valleys in the Pyrenees, a wide glacial meadow at about 1,620 m where the Aragón Subordán river meanders in lazy zigzags past terrain rich in megalithic remains, including a dolmen.

highlights.title: Cuello del Infierno and the Ibones Azules
highlights.body: Above Respomuso the route climbs to the Collado de Tebarray (about 2,771 m), the section's highest point, beneath the 3,082 m Picos del Infierno, then drops through the Piedrafita gorge past the blue tarns of the Ibones Azules.

highlights.title: Refugio de Respomuso
highlights.body: A staffed high-mountain refuge at about 2,200 m beside the Respomuso reservoir, the standard overnight above Sallent de Gállego and the launch point for the Infierno passes.

highlights.title: Collado de Añisclo and the Pineta cirque
highlights.body: From Refugio de Góriz the GR11 climbs over the grassy Collado de Añisclo at 2,453 m before plunging into the Circo de Pineta, a vast glacial amphitheatre walled by the north face of Monte Perdido.

highlights.title: Refugio de Góriz (2,200 m)
highlights.body: The only mountain hut inside Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, Góriz sits at 2,200 m and is reachable solely on foot, making it the classic base beneath Monte Perdido and the staging point for this whole section.

highlights.title: Llanos de La Larri balcony
highlights.body: On the climb out of Pineta the trail reaches the Llanos de La Larri, a hanging natural balcony from which the full magnitude of the north face of Monte Perdido is revealed.

highlights.title: Vallibierna lakes beneath Aneto
highlights.body: The high stage to Refugio de Cap de Llauset passes the twin Vallibierna ibones (2,428 m and 2,475 m) under the granite giants of Aneto (3,404 m), Tempestades and Margalida.

highlights.title: Estany de Sant Maurici & Els Encantats
highlights.body: The descent from Port de Ràtera (2,534m) ends at Estany de Sant Maurici (1,915m), set at the foot of the twin rocky spires of Els Encantats, the iconic image of the national park.

highlights.title: Circ de Colomèrs lake country
highlights.body: Above La Restanca the trail crosses into the Circ de Colomèrs, one of the largest concentrations of glacial lakes in the Pyrenees, threading past Cap de Port, Port de Caldes and Lac Major de Colomèrs.

highlights.title: Montardó from Oelhacrestada
highlights.body: From the Oelhacrestada col (2,475m) a short side-trip of about 40 minutes summits Montardó (2,833m), the iconic peak presiding over the Aigüestortes lakes.

highlights.title: Coll de Tudela & Pica d'Estats panorama
highlights.body: On the long climb out of Tavascán toward Àreu, the wide Coll de Tudela (2,239m) opens views across the Vallferrera to Pica d'Estats (3,143m), Catalonia's highest peak.

highlights.title: Vall de Núria sanctuary
highlights.body: The GR11 climbs to the Santuari de Núria, a Marian pilgrimage shrine at 1,967m set in a glacial cirque and reached only on foot or by a historic rack railway up from Queralbs.

highlights.title: Coll de Noucreus frontier ridge
highlights.body: Between Núria and Ulldeter the trail rides the France-Spain frontier ridge to the Collado de Noucreus at 2,790m, threading a string of 2,800m peaks like Pic de la Fossa del Gegant (2,808m) and the Vaca summits.

highlights.title: Refugi d'Ulldeter and the source of the Ter
highlights.body: The Refugi d'Ulldeter sits at 2,220m beneath Bastiments (2,881m), Gra de Fajol and Pic de la Dona, right beside the headwaters of the river Ter.

highlights.title: Sant Aniol d'Aguja in the Alta Garrotxa
highlights.body: Near the end the route drops into the Alta Garrotxa's dense Mediterranean woodland of evergreen and cork oaks to the remote Romanesque chapel of Sant Aniol d'Aguja (446m), with a drinking fountain and an ideal camping spot beside the old ruined refuge.

highlights.title: La Rhune (Larrun)
highlights.body: At 905 m, the highest peak on the Basque coast gives a 360° panorama from San Sebastián along the Atlantic to Biarritz and inland to the Pyrenees, reached on foot near the GR10 or by the historic 1924 rack railway from Col de Saint-Ignace.

highlights.title: Crête d'Iparla
highlights.body: Above Bidarray the GR10 runs along a line of dramatic sandstone cliffs to the 1044 m Pic d'Iparla, where griffon vultures nest and soar overhead and the summit opens a view from the Basque beaches to the Hautes-Pyrénées.

highlights.title: Ainhoa village
highlights.body: Classified among the Plus Beaux Villages de France, this 13th-century bastide on the pilgrim road to Santiago lines a single street of white timber-framed Basque houses with red and green shutters.

highlights.title: Forêt d'Iraty beech forest
highlights.body: Near the Chalets d'Iraty the GR10 enters Europe's largest beech forest, a 17,300-hectare hêtraie straddling the Spanish border at 1,300–1,500 m, with a stopover gîte at the edge of the woods.

highlights.title: Cirque de Lescun
highlights.body: From Lescun — the highest commune in the Pyrénées-Atlantiques — the trail opens onto the limestone amphitheatre nicknamed the "Dolomites of the Pyrenees," ringed by the Aiguille d'Ansabère, the Table des Trois Rois and the great Billare.

highlights.title: Chemin de la Mâture
highlights.body: Above Etsaut you walk a 1,200 m ledge hewn into the rock face in 1772 to drag timber down for French naval masts, threading almost under the cliff with the Fort du Portalet perched opposite.

highlights.title: Lac Gentau & Pic du Midi d'Ossau
highlights.body: The high variant past the Refuge d'Ayous looks straight onto Lac Gentau, where the unmistakable horn of the Pic du Midi d'Ossau is mirrored in the water before the descent to Gabas.

highlights.title: La Pierre Saint-Martin karst
highlights.body: Climbing from the canyons of Sainte-Engrâce, the GR10 crosses the vast karst plateau of La Pierre Saint-Martin — 140 km² of limestone holding some of the deepest caves in the world — beneath the pyramid of the Pic d'Anie.

highlights.title: Lac de Gaube and the Vignemale
highlights.body: Above Cauterets the GR10 reaches the emblematic Lac de Gaube at 1,725 m, set directly beneath the 3,298 m north face of the Vignemale, the highest peak of the French Pyrenees.

highlights.title: Cirque de Gavarnie and its waterfall
highlights.body: This stretch passes the UNESCO-listed Cirque de Gavarnie (part of the Pyrénées-Mont Perdu World Heritage Site), whose Gavarnie Falls drop about 422 m as the highest waterfall in mainland France.

highlights.title: Lac d'Estaing and the Col d'Ilhéou
highlights.body: Out of Arrens-Marsous the route climbs past the much-loved Lac d'Estaing before crossing the Col d'Ilhéou at 2,242 m down to the Lac and Refuge d'Ilhéou.

highlights.title: Néouvielle lakes above Barèges
highlights.body: Beyond Barèges the trail climbs to the Col de Madamète (2,508 m) and enters the Néouvielle nature reserve, threading the Aubert and Aumar lakes amid some of the highest mountain-pine (pins à crochets) forests in Europe down to the Lac de l'Oule.

highlights.title: Lac d'Oô and its 275m waterfall
highlights.body: The trail reaches Lac d'Oô, the second most-visited natural site in the Pyrenees, where one of the range's highest waterfalls plunges some 275 m down the steep slopes across the lake.

highlights.title: Bagnères-de-Luchon spa town
highlights.body: The section passes through Bagnères-de-Luchon, a historic Pyrenean spa town and the main resupply and rest point before the wild, infrastructure-light stages east into Ariège.

highlights.title: Refuge de l'Étang d'Araing under Pic de Crabère
highlights.body: The section ends at the Refuge de l'Étang d'Araing (1,965 m), sat beside its lake at the foot of Pic de Crabère where the GR10 meets the HRP, staffed with 52 places from June to September.

highlights.title: Refuge de l'Étang d'Araing
highlights.body: This staffed refuge (the Jacques Husson hut, ~1,965 m, 53 places) sits right on its namesake lake beneath the cliffs of the Pic de Crabère, a classic GR10 night-stop at the foot of the climb out of Couserans.

highlights.title: Cascade d'Ars above Aulus-les-Bains
highlights.body: Dropping into Aulus-les-Bains, the trail passes near the Cascade d'Ars, one of the highest waterfalls in the massif at 246 m over three tiers, with the top fall plunging some 110 m in free fall.

highlights.title: The Bentaillou mines descent to Eylie
highlights.body: Below the Étang d'Araing the GR10 drops past the abandoned Bentaillou mine workings before reaching the hamlet of Eylie-d'en-Haut, a stark industrial-heritage interlude in an otherwise wild stretch.

highlights.title: Étangs de Bassiès
highlights.body: The GR10 threads the wild granite cirque of Bassiès, where glacial lakes — the Étang Majeur, Étang Long and Étang d'Escalès — string out over roughly 2.5 km at around 1,600 m before the refuge at 1,650 m.

highlights.title: Hot springs at Mérens-les-Vals
highlights.body: A short 20–30 minute walk up the GR10 from Mérens-les-Vals leads to free, wild sulphurous hot-spring pools of stone basins fed by water at around 37–40°C.

highlights.title: Refuge des Bésines & the high border country
highlights.body: The stage out of Mérens-les-Vals climbs to the FFCAM Refuge des Bésines at 2,104 m, crossing the Porteille des Bésines at 2,333 m on the edge of the Catalan Pyrenees natural park.

highlights.title: Pic du Canigou (2,784 m), the Catalans' sacred mountain
highlights.body: From the Cortalets refuge the GR10 climbs toward Pic du Canigou (2,784 m), the sacred mountain of the Catalans, visible across the whole Roussillon plain and the high point of this entire stretch.

highlights.title: Mantet, an isolated village in its own nature reserve
highlights.body: Reached over the Col du Pal (about 2,294 m), Mantet sits at 1,540 m as one of the most isolated villages in the Pyrénées-Orientales, ringed by the 3,028-hectare Réserve Naturelle de Mantet that covers 94% of the commune.

highlights.title: Refuge du Ras de la Carança on the river
highlights.body: The Refuge du Ras de la Carança stands on the banks of the Carança river at a GR10 crossroads, with 13 dormitory beds, meals and showers during its mid-May to mid-September guarding period, and tent space alongside.

highlights.title: The Carança gorge footbridges below the trail
highlights.body: Below the refuge the Carança river carves the vertiginous Gorges de la Carança, a canyon of rope bridges, suspended walkways, ladders and cliff-cut cornices worth a detour from Thuès-Entre-Valls if you have the time and a head for heights.

highlights.title: The Paso de Mahoma
highlights.body: The exposed knife-edge stride onto the summit block.

highlights.title: The Aneto glacier
highlights.body: The largest glacier in the Pyrenees, shrinking fast.
