objective.name: GR11
objective.summary: The Senda Pirenaica traces the Spanish Pyrenees coast to coast — from Hondarribia on the Atlantic to Cap de Creus on the Mediterranean, waymarked red-and-white the whole way.

guide.heading: Distance, elevation & duration
guide.body: Roughly 847.4 km with about 38,193 m of cumulative ascent over 46 stages.

blocks.label: Moderate–Hard
blocks.note: Long days · up to 1,580 m ascent
Mid-June to mid-September; July and August are the most reliable. The high cols hold snow into early summer.

guide.heading: Where to sleep
guide.body: The trail averages roughly one refugio a day. Staffed huts are the backbone — dormitory beds, a hot meal, a packed lunch, blankets & local information; reckon on around €26 for the bed, €22 for dinner and €11 for breakfast, and bring a sleeping-bag liner since bags aren't supplied. In valleys you'll also find albergues, pensions, hostals & casas rurales, and unstaffed free huts cover the more isolated stretches. Book ahead from mid-July to late August when refuges fill fast; solo walkers can often find a space without it. Wild camping is illegal, but bivouacking — a small tent pitched at dusk and struck at dawn, away from habitation — is tolerated, usually above 1,800–2,100 m; note that camping is banned in Aigüestortes and is restricted in Ordesa to the area by the Góriz refuge.

guide.heading: Water on the trail
guide.body: Water is dependable across most of the GR11 — village fountains, taps near refuges & mountain springs let you refill often, and unstaffed huts usually have drinking water. The exception is the far eastern end in Catalonia, approaching the Mediterranean, where sources thin out and temperatures can top 30°C; carry extra through there. Treat what you collect: much of the easily reached water sits below grazing land and free-range cattle, so a filter or other treatment is strongly advised. Two to three litres of capacity suits most stages, with more on the dry Catalan stretches.

guide.heading: Staying safe
guide.body: The main risk is afternoon weather: storms build over the high country in summer, often in the evening and night, so cross exposed passes early and be down before they hit. The GR11 climbs many passes with scree, boulderfields & easy scrambling — about as hard as an inexperienced walker would want under a heavy pack — and snow can linger on passes above 2,500 m into early summer, hiding markers and demanding an ice axe and crampons. Fog can drop visibility to a few metres even on a marked route. The emergency number Spain-wide is 112, reaching mountain rescue, but reception is patchy in remote valleys, so leave your route and expected arrival with someone, and use the staffed refuges as shelter and a source of current conditions.

guide.heading: From beech to bare granite
guide.body: The GR11 climbs through the same vegetation belts again and again as it crosses each massif: oak and beech woods low down, the great Irati forest in Navarre, then conifers thinning out around 2,300 m, alpine meadow above that, and exposed rock above roughly 2,500 m. Watch the high ground for izards (the Pyrenean chamois, sarrio in Spanish) on the slopes and marmots whistling from boulder fields — both common above the treeline. Overhead you'll see griffon and Egyptian vultures, golden eagles, and, with luck over Ordesa y Monte Perdido, the bearded vulture or quebrantahuesos — the "bone-breaker," with a near three-metre wingspan, that lives almost entirely on bone and has its European stronghold here.

guide.heading: Eat the valley you're in
guide.body: Mountain food on the GR11 is shepherd's food, and it changes region by region. In the Basque Country and Navarre look for sheep's-milk cheeses — Idiazabal, and the Navarrese curados — and river trout (trucha) off the menu in valley villages. In Aragon, migas reward the hungry: stale bread crumbled and fried with garlic, chorizo, and bacon, sometimes with grapes — a meal built for a working day in the hills. Refuge dinners are usually a fixed three-course menú; stock up on cheese and cured meat in the larger valley towns like Jaca, since long stretches between resupply are normal.

guide.heading: Drier as you go east, storms in the afternoon
guide.body: Walk the GR11 west to east and you feel the climate change with you: the Atlantic-facing Basque hills are mist-prone and green, while by the Mediterranean end rain is rare and afternoons can climb past 30°C. July to September is the season — the high passes are usually clear of snow and the days long. The summer pattern to plan around is the thunderstorm: hot, settled mornings that build cloud through the day, with storms most often breaking in the late afternoon, evening, or overnight. Start early, aim to be off the high cols by early afternoon, and treat a clear dawn as the time to make your distance.

sections.name: Basque Country & Navarre
sections.tagline: From Cabo Higuer, passing through forests and coastal hills.
sections.summary: From Cabo Higuer, passing through forests and coastal hills.

guide.heading: Green hills before the high cols
guide.body: This is the Atlantic end of the GR11, where the walking is rolling rather than alpine: ridges and meadows between caseríos, with the day's work coming from accumulated up-and-down over long stages rather than altitude. From Cabo Higuer at sea level the route climbs gradually inland, crossing low passes — Lizarrieta, Urkiaga, Argibiela — before the ground steepens and rises into the Roncal valley. By the col into Aragón at Zuriza you are above 1,200 m, and the real climbs are about to begin.

guide.heading: Beech forest most of the way
guide.body: European beech shades stage after stage here, from the Quinto Real woods above Elizondo to the long descent into Isaba. East of Hiriberri the route skirts the Irati Forest — the second-largest and best-preserved beech-fir forest in Europe, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve since 2024, where silver fir joins the beech and oak, birch and willow fill the gaps. In autumn the holly and ferns come into their own; the higher passes give way to open pasture.

guide.heading: Baztan, shepherds & witches
guide.body: You cross into Navarre on the first day and spend the stretch in deep Basque country — euskera place names, stone caseríos, and the bordas (shepherds' huts) of the Quinto Real and Aezkoa valleys. Elizondo is the capital of the Baztan, the valley whose neighbour Zugarramurdi gave the Inquisition its most famous witch trials between 1609 and 1614. Ochagavía and Isaba keep the older rhythm of the Salazar and Roncal valleys, where transhumant shepherding shaped the high pastures the trail crosses.

guide.heading: The wet, green end of the range
guide.body: This is the Atlantic flank of the Pyrenees, closest to the Bay of Biscay, and it is green because it is wet — cloud and rain arrive off the sea more often than further east. Fog is the practical hazard, not heat: several stages warn that mist in the upper pasture zones, between Urkiaga and the Abodi passes, can make orientation hard. The walking season runs roughly March to November, with summer the most settled.

callouts.body: On the Hiriberri-to-Ochagavía leg over the Abodi ridge, take care in fog not to lose the waymarks between Paso Tapla and the Paso de las Alforjas, where the open high ground offers few landmarks.
Plan your water: the Ochagavía-to-Isaba stage has no drinking fountains until almost the very end, so fill up before leaving the village.
Livestock-guardian dogs (mastines) on the open pastures can be aggressive near their flocks and vipers bask in sunny rocky spots, so give dogs a wide, calm berth and watch where you step.

sections.name: Aragonese Pyrenees
sections.tagline: Higher, rugged alpine terrain, passing through locations like Candanchú and the Respomuso Hut.
sections.summary: Higher, rugged alpine terrain, passing through locations like Candanchú and the Respomuso Hut.

guide.heading: Flysch gives way to granite
guide.body: The early stages cut across the folded flysch and limestone of the Inner Sierras — alternating sandstone and marl that bends into shapes like the Paso de la Oreja's anticline above Lizara. By the Tena valley the rock turns harder and the ground steps up into true high country: glacier-carved cirques, ibones (mountain lakes) like Anayet and Bachimaña, and passes above 2,700 m at Tebarray and Brazato. This is the first stretch where the trail stays high for days rather than dropping to a village each night.

guide.heading: Through the pine line into open meadow
guide.body: Below about 1,600 m you walk through beech and silver fir; higher up the subalpine belt is pino negro (Pinus uncinata), the mountain pine that holds the slopes up to roughly 2,400 m, often over a carpet of rhododendron and bilberry. Cross the treeline — around 2,200–2,450 m here — and the forest gives out to alpine meadow and juniper, the grazed grassland that fills Aguas Tuertas and the high pastures. The meadows are at their fullest and most flowered in the weeks just after snowmelt.

guide.heading: A valley run on grass
guide.body: The Tena valley, with Sallent de Gállego as its main town, was for centuries organised around its pastures — managed by the Junta General de la Val de Tena and split into the quiñones of Sallent, Panticosa and Partacua. Pyrenean life has mingled with pastoralism for six or seven thousand years, flocks climbing to the high meadows in late spring and coming down in autumn. The grazed grass underfoot at Aguas Tuertas and Respomuso is the working surface of that history, not a backdrop to it.

guide.heading: Be over the pass before the build-up
guide.body: Summer days are hot with hard sun, but in July and August thunderstorms build through the afternoon, especially when a southerly wind pushes moist air up from the plains — the standard rule is to be off exposed ridges and high passes before 2 pm. Snow lingers on the highest cols well into summer: Tebarray (2,771 m) can hold it until mid-August, and Brazato into July, both steep enough to want an ice axe when iced. Start early, and treat the high passes as the part of the day that decides the rest.

callouts.body: The Collado de Tebarray (about 2,771 m) above Respomuso can hold snow into mid-August, with a final pitch near 40 degrees where an ice axe is advised in early-season conditions; cables are installed on the steepest sections.
Aguas Tuertas ends at a free, unstaffed refuge sleeping only 4-5 people with closed facilities onward, so carry a sleeping bag, mat and food for the next day rather than relying on services.

On the high open passes around Toronez and Bernera, navigate carefully in fog to avoid an unintended descent toward Estanés, and expect some not-always-easy stream crossings such as the Barranco de la Rueda.

sections.name: Ordesa & High Country
sections.tagline: Includes the scenic Ordesa National Park and the high limestone, passing through Góriz and Viadós.
sections.summary: Includes the scenic Ordesa National Park and the high limestone, passing through Góriz and Viadós.

guide.heading: Limestone cut by water
guide.body: This is karst country: the Ordesa canyon was carved by the río Arazas into Cretaceous and Eocene limestone, and the same rock holds Europe's deepest gorges on the Spanish side. Above them sits Monte Perdido, whose north-face glacier is one of the southernmost in Europe and retreating fast. The walking ranges from forested valley floors to high passes above 2,500m, including the drop off Collado de Añisclo into Pineta — roughly 1,200m of descent in about 2.5km, the steepest sustained loss on the GR11.

guide.heading: Beech below, endemics on the walls
guide.body: Climbing the Ordesa valley you pass through the Bosque de las Hayas, a beech forest, before the trees give way to black pine and then open alpine grassland higher up. The park holds over 1,500 plant species, and the limestone cliffs and scree shelter Pyrenean endemics found almost nowhere else — among them Ramonda myconi and Borderea pyrenaica. The richest displays cling to the rock walls and ledges rather than the path itself.

guide.heading: Clear mornings, building afternoons
guide.body: In July and August the pattern is reliable enough to plan around: clear mornings, then thunderstorms building in the afternoon when warm, moist air pushes up from the south. The high passes here — Chistau at 2,592m, Vallibierna at 2,710m — are exposed and dangerous in a storm. Start early, aim to be off the cols by midday, and treat dark clouds or thunder as a signal to drop down rather than push on.

callouts.body: The Góriz–Pineta stage ends in what guides call probably the toughest descent on the whole GR11 — roughly 1,200 m of drop in about 2.5 km over ledges where you must use your hands, so wet rock or fatigue here is genuinely dangerous.

The Cuello de Vallibierna pass tops out at 2,710 m and can still hold snow in early summer, with the boulder field below the pass swallowing the path — a serious problem in fog or with a snowpack masking the route.

This is high, remote, and committing terrain: several stages cross passes above 2,300–2,710 m with long climbs (Parzán–Biados gains nearly 1,600 m over 21.7 km) and few services between huts, so build in afternoon-storm margins and don't rely on resupply between refuges.

sections.name: Andorra & Pallars High Country
sections.tagline: Traverses granite landscapes in Andorra and the Catalan Pyrenees, including areas like Colomèrs.
sections.summary: Traverses granite landscapes in Andorra and the Catalan Pyrenees, including areas like Colomèrs.

guide.heading: Lakeside grass to scree and pine
guide.body: Around Restanca, Colomèrs and Sant Maurici the high basins hold alpine meadow and lakeside grass below bare granite, with the Els Encantats outcrop standing over Sant Maurici. The descent into Pallars Sobirà passes through pine woodland and old terraced pastures around villages like Estaon and Boldís, where abandoned barns mark land once grazed hard. Toward Malniu and the Cerdanya the ground turns drier and scrubbier as Mediterranean influence creeps in from the south.

guide.heading: Pastoral Pallars, then Andorra's two faces
guide.body: The valley stages thread Pallars Sobirà villages — Espot, Estaon, Tavascan, Àreu — past summer barns and half-empty hamlets that record a pastoral economy now largely gone. Andorra shows two faces in quick succession: the ski-resort development of Arinsal and Encamp, then the Madriu-Perafita-Claror valley, a UNESCO World Heritage cultural landscape (2004) where summer settlements, stone tracks and a communal land system reaching back to the 13th century survive. Refugi de l'Illa sits inside that protected valley.

guide.heading: A July–September window, snow lingers high
guide.body: This stretch walks best from July to September, when the huts are staffed and the high ports are clear. In early summer snow holds on the highest cols — Port de Ratera (2,534m) and especially Port de Baiau (2,757m), where an ice axe and crampons can be needed and the boulder slope above is loose. Later in the season the lower valleys around Tavascan and the approach to Malniu turn hot and dry, so the risk shifts from snow to heat and water.

callouts.body: In early summer you may hit lingering snow on the high cols of the Colomèrs section, between Port de Oelhacrestada (2,475m) and the stage high point at Port de Caldes (2,568m), and at Port de Ràtera (2,534m) above Sant Maurici.

The Tavascán-to-Àreu leg is a long, demanding stage climbing about 1,225m over 14.7km to Coll de Tudela, where slippery ground can be treacherous and the wooded final descent has confusing track crossings.

sections.name: Eastern Pyrenees
sections.tagline: Descending through forests and valleys towards the Mediterranean coast at Cap de Creus.
sections.summary: Descending through forests and valleys towards the Mediterranean coast at Cap de Creus.

guide.heading: From the border ridge to the sea
guide.body: This stretch loses the high Pyrenees in stages. Above Núria the GR11 still walks the frontier crest from the Collado de Noucreus past peaks near 2,800m, but past Ulldeter it drops into the green Ripollès valleys and then the lower Alta Garrotxa, before the Albera range carries it down to the Mediterranean at Cap de Creus. The last day finishes barely above sea level on wind-scoured rock, a long way in feel from the alpine cols where the section began.

guide.heading: Pine, cork oak, then scrub
guide.body: The vegetation tracks the descent. Steep pine forest above Planoles and Queralbs gives way to mixed mountain woodland around Molló and the dense Mediterranean woods of the Alta Garrotxa, then to the cork oak and pine of the Albera range above Requessens. By Cap de Creus the trees are gone, replaced by low wind-pruned scrub on the rock — the tramuntana shapes what grows as much as it shapes the stone.

guide.heading: Sanctuaries, monasteries and the Cerdanya
guide.body: The walking threads a chain of religious sites: the mountain sanctuary of Núria, reached on foot or by the Cremallera rack railway running up from Ribes de Freser since 1931; the Romanesque chapel of Sant Aniol d'Aguja, with a monastery founded in 859; and Sant Pere de Rodes, a Catalan Romanesque monastery on the Serra de Rodes whose church was consecrated in 1022. Puigcerdà sits in the broad Cerdanya, a wide upland valley split between Spain and France.

guide.heading: Snow up high, tramuntana down low
guide.body: The legs from Molló east to Cap de Creus can be walked year-round, but the high stages around Núria and Ulldeter hold snow from roughly mid-December into late June, where an ice axe and crampons can be needed on the ridge. Lower down the danger flips from cold to heat and water: stages like Talaixà to Albanyà list heat as the main difficulty. On the coast the tramuntana, a northwest wind that can reach gale force, dominates the final approach.

callouts.body: The high frontier traverse from Núria over Coll de Noucreus (2,790m) to Ulldeter stays above 2,700m for hours and can hold snow into early summer, where the guide warns an ice axe and crampons could be necessary.

In the closing Alta Garrotxa stage to Albanyà, water becomes complicated to find after the Sant Aniol fountain, and the next fountain at Can Nou may be dry, so fill up before the long descent through the woods.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The trail starts at Cabo Higuer with the Cantabrian Sea below, then climbs gradually inland through Hondarribia and Irún and over the Ursain col to the Endara reservoir before dropping to Bera in Navarre. It is a long day — around 30 km — and the one thing that matters is route-finding through the streets of Hondarribia and Irún; once you are out of town the waymarking is excellent.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long stage of nearly 30 km that strings together a series of low cols — Lizarrieta among them — across meadows and past stone caseríos before descending to Elizondo in the Baztan valley. There is no single hard climb; the day's difficulty is the sheer accumulated up-and-down, so pace it and use the bar-shop at the Lizarrieta pass.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From Elizondo the route leaves the Atlantic hills and climbs steadily into the beech forests of the Quinto Real, a gradual ascent rather than a steep one, to the Puerto de Urkiaga. Plan your night before you set off — Urkiaga itself has no accommodation, with the nearest beds at Eugi, well off the trail.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A walk through the heart of the Quinto Real, climbing past bordas and hunting posts through old beech woods that open into broad pasture at the high point before the drop to Burguete. Watch the open upper sections in fog, where the grassland gives few handrails.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: An easy 17 km on tracks and forest paths, through beech then pine over the Usateguieta col and down into the Aezkoa valley, finishing across the Irati river on a medieval bridge into Hiriberri. A gentle day and a good one to recover legs after the cols.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The route skirts the edge of the Irati Forest and climbs over the open Sierra de Abodi — Ollokiate and Tapla passes, the Abodi vertex at nearly 1,500 m — before a steep descent past the Muskilda sanctuary into Ochagavía. The high traverse is exposed, so the thing to watch is fog between the passes; otherwise the views run the length of the Pyrenees.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A mostly track-walking day from the Salazar valley to the Roncal, leisurely through pine and box until the land opens below Kakueta and the peaks come into view, then a steep beech-forest descent that tests the knees into Isaba. Carry water — there are no fountains until almost the end of the stage.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A short 11 km half-day that leaves Isaba up the Belabarze ravine through thick forest, eases into cattle pasture, then crosses the Collado de Argibiela (Puerto de los Navarros) to leave Navarre for Aragón and descend gently to Zuriza. Note that the Zuriza campsite is closed, though a free two-floor shelter remains below it.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A sustained climb out of Zuriza up the Petraficha ravine to its col at 1,963 m, then down through the Aragón Subordán valley and up into the meadow at Aguas Tuertas. The night here is a tiny free cabin sleeping four or five with no services, so carry food, mat and a bag — and plan for the next day too, as resupply is thin until Sallent de Gállego.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A shorter day climbing the glacial Sarrios valley over a string of passes — Escalé, Toronez d'as Cabretas, Bernera at 2,102 m — past the Ibón de Estanés to the staffed Lizara refuge. The one to watch is Toronez d'as Cabretas in fog: follow the signs carefully so you don't drop toward the lake, which is walled off on that side.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: High alpine walking over the Collado del Bozo and Collado de Esper (2,262 m), past Tuca Blanca, before descending the ski slopes into Candanchú. The crux is the Paso de la Oreja, a grassy strip suspended above a drop with a fixed rope — straightforward in the dry, but treat it with real care if there's any snow.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long day, nearly 24 km, past the Canal Roya refuge and up to the Ibones de Anayet at 2,225 m beneath the Lecherines, then a long drop into Sallent de Gállego. The thing to time is the Culivillas ravine on the descent — the stream can be hard to cross during snowmelt — and the lake section can lose its markings under late snow.

stages.name: Sallent de Gállego → Refugio de Respomuso

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The shortest stage of the stretch but relentlessly uphill: a steady 900 m climb up the Aguas Limpias valley, through the Paso del Onso cliffs and past waterfalls into the Ordesa-Viñamala reserve, finishing at the Respomuso dam and refuge near 2,200 m. No real technical difficulty — just pace the continuous ascent, and book the refuge ahead in summer.

stages.name: Refugio de Respomuso → Balneario de Panticosa

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The high point of the whole section: a climb past the Llena Cantal and Bachimaña lakes over the Collado de Tebarray (2,771 m), then a steep gorge descent through pine to Balneario de Panticosa. Tebarray is the crux — unstable rock and a final 40° pitch that can hold snow until mid-August, so carry an ice axe and check conditions before committing.

stages.name: Balneario de Panticosa → San Nicolás de Bujaruelo

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long, big-feeling day over the Cuello Alto de Brazato (2,566 m), with the Vignemale massif in view, past the Brazato and Batanes lakes and down into the broad Ara valley to San Nicolás de Bujaruelo. Brazato can hold snow into July and may want an ice axe early in the season — otherwise it's the climb and the 7-plus hours that ask the most of you.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: Down the Bujaruelo valley and through the Garganta de los Navarros gorge — including the Paso de la Escala, an airy ledge fitted with a safety line — then up the whole Ordesa valley past the Cola de Caballo waterfall to Góriz. The long, steady climb through the national park is the heart of the day; the Paso de la Escala is the one spot that demands care.

stages.name: Refugio de Góriz → Refugio de Pineta

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From Góriz over Collado de Añisclo, then the long way down to Pineta — about 1,200m of descent in roughly 2.5km, the steepest sustained drop on the GR11, with ledges to clamber over near the bottom. The descent is the whole challenge: it is hard on the knees and slow, so leave Góriz with time in hand.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A steep climb out of the Pineta cirque to the Llanos de la Larri balcony, with the north face of Monte Perdido in view, then over the Collado de las Coronetas (2,159m) and down to Parzán. No real technical difficulty — the early climb is simply long, so pace it.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long day on tracks for the climb to the Collado de Urdiceto (2,314m), past its dam and power station, then a comfortable descent through changing valleys to Viadós (Refugio de Biadós). It is the distance and 1,500m-plus of ascent that make it demanding, not the terrain — the route is well waymarked throughout.

stages.name: Refugio de Biados → Puente de San Jaime

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: Up to the Puerto de Chistau (2,592m), with a row of 3,000m peaks across the valley, then a long descent through the Estós valley past Refugio de Estós to Puente de San Jaime. Water is easy with streams all the way; the main concern is snow lingering on the pass in early summer.

stages.name: Puente de San Jaime → Refugio de Cap de Llauset

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: Ten easy kilometres along the río Ésera to Coronas, then high-mountain ground where the path fades among boulders on the climb to the Cuello de Vallibierna (2,710m), with Aneto and the Maladeta in view. The boulder-field upper section is the crux — snow can hold there into early summer, and Coronas is a sensible bailout if conditions turn.

stages.name: Refugio de Cap de Llauset → Refugio de Conangles

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A climb to the Collada dels Estanyets to start, then mostly downhill past the Anglios lakes and through the Salenques beech forest to Conangles, crossing from Aragon into Catalonia at the Noguera Ribagorzana. The opening climb is the only real effort; the rest is a long, scenic descent.

stages.name: Refugio de Conangles → Refugio de la Restanca

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A short stage on good paths over Port de Rius (2,355m), passing Estany de Rius before the final climb to the Restanca dam, where the refuge sits at 2,010m. Streams and lakes run the whole way, so water is never a worry — the only fuzzy part is the less-marked pull up to the refuge at the end.

stages.name: Refugio de la Restanca → Refugio de Colomers

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: Seven kilometres but two high cols — Port d'Oelhacrestada (2,475m), with Montardo rising beside it, then Port de Caldes (2,568m) — dropping into the lake-strewn Circ de Colomèrs. It is short and spectacular but books out: reserve Colomèrs well ahead in July and August, and expect snow on the cols in early summer.

stages.name: Refugio de Colomers → Refugio Ernest Mallafré

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: An easy lake-to-lake stage over Port de Ratera (2,534m) on ground shared with the Carros de Foc circuit, descending past Estany de Ratera to Sant Maurici under the cliffs of Els Encantats. The one thing to watch is lingering snow on Port de Ratera early in the season; otherwise this is one of the most scenic and forgiving days on the trail.

stages.name: Estany de Sant Maurici → Espot → La Guingueta

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long descent away from the national park — leaving Sant Maurici under Els Encantats and dropping over 1,200m through Espot to La Guingueta on the Noguera Pallaresa. The walking is straightforward; the day is about losing height and the knees, and trading high lakes for valley villages.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A quiet, climbing stage through Dorve and over Coll de Montcaubo into remote Pallars Sobirà, past abandoned villages and barns well off the tourist line. It is solitary and steep in places — carry water, as the route stays away from reliable sources for long stretches.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A gentler valley day through pine and old pastoral country, passing the Nibrós barns and other abandoned settlements on the way to Tavascan. The terrain is less rugged than the days either side; the main thing is the long, steady rhythm of a transitional stage between villages.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A big six-hour day climbing out of Tavascan — the first pull above the village is brutal and eroded, with chains in places — over Boldís and Coll de Tudela toward Vall Ferrera and Àreu, under the emblematic Pica d'Estats. Pace the early climb and filter water carefully, as fountains low down can run cloudy.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A demanding climb up the Vall Ferrera through streams, lakes and high meadows that give way to rock as you near Refugi de Baiau, the small hut in its amphitheatre of 2,900m peaks. It gains over 1,300m with almost no descent, so it is all uphill — arrive with enough left for the next morning's port.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The crossing into Andorra over Port de Baiau (2,757m) — steep, loose boulders and scree where an ice axe and crampons may be obligatory in early summer — then a long 1,900m descent past Estany Negre and under Comapedrosa to Arans. The port is the defining challenge of the whole section: check conditions and start early.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A quieter Andorran day over Coll d'Ordino, through pine forest and small villages with a steep descent into Encamp, passing the Romanesque chapel of Sant Romà de les Bons. After the high passes this is a recovery stage, though the contrast of ski-resort development in the valley is jarring.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long 20km climb up the Madriu-Perafita-Claror valley — a UNESCO World Heritage pastoral landscape of torrents, stone tracks and summer settlements — to Refugi de l'Illa beside its reservoir. The gradient is steady rather than steep; the day is about distance and the slow gain into one of Andorra's protected high valleys.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A high traverse that never drops below 2,000m, crossing the Vallcivera and Engorgs cols out of Andorra into the Cerdanya toward Refugi de Malniu. Short by the standards of this section, it stays exposed and alpine — watch for afternoon heat and thinning water as the ground turns drier on the southern side.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A gentle valley-floor day off the high mountain, dropping from the Malniu refuge through Guils de Cerdanya into Puigcerdà on the wide floor of the Cerdanya. The walking is easy; what matters is that this is a resupply and transport hub, a good place to restock before the climb back toward Núria.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A transition stage across the Cerdanya from Puigcerdà to Planoles on tracks and quiet paths, much of it the old pilgrim route once used to reach Núria. Undemanding underfoot — the point is repositioning for the big climb that follows.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A hard climbing day: about 1,440m of ascent through steep pine forest, down to Queralbs, then up through dramatic gorges past the Cua de Cavall waterfall to the sanctuary of Núria. The accumulated elevation is the challenge, so pace the long final ascent.

stages.name: Santuario de Núria → Refugio de Ulldeter

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A classic high-mountain crossing: roughly 1,000m of ascent from Núria to the Collado de Noucreus at 2,790m, then a ridge walk along the frontier crest below peaks near 2,800m to the Ulldeter refuge. Snow lingers here into late June, when an ice axe and crampons can be needed on the col.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The long descent off the high Pyrenees, leaving the alpine headwaters of the Ter for the greener Ripollès valleys and dropping through Setcases down to Molló. Mostly downhill and straightforward — the day marks the real change from high mountain to mid-mountain country.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A varied stage out of Molló into warmer, Mediterranean-influenced hills, with a clear shift in landscape as you enter the Alta Garrotxa toward the hamlet of Talaixà. The mid-mountain woodland and changing vegetation are the day's character; water becomes something to plan around from here on.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long descending day of about 19km through the Alta Garrotxa, passing the Romanesque chapel of Sant Aniol d'Aguja, climbing to Coll Roig and Coll de Bassegoda below the Garrotxa's highest peak, then dropping to Albanyà. Heat is the main difficulty and water is unreliable after Sant Aniol — carry enough.

stages.name: Albanyà → Maçanet de Cabrenys → La Vajol

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A day through the wooded hills of the Alt Empordà from Albanyà via Maçanet de Cabrenys to La Vajol. Forest tracks and quiet country roads make for steady walking; the interest is the villages and the increasingly Mediterranean feel rather than any single climb.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The trail enters the Albera range, crossing from La Vajol via La Jonquera to the sanctuary of Mare de Déu de Requessens, set in forested hills below the ruined medieval castle. Cork oak and pine woods define the walking, and the remote sanctuary makes a quiet end to the day.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A short stage on dirt tracks through the oak-lined Albera, leaving Requessens for Vilamaniscle with the first real sweeping views of the Mediterranean opening ahead. Easy underfoot — the sea coming into sight is the moment that matters.

stages.name: Vilamaniscle → Llançà → El Port de la Selva

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A descent out of the hills to the coast at Llançà, then along to El Port de la Selva, with the Romanesque monastery of Sant Pere de Rodes high on the Serra de Rodes above the route. The sea is the reward; the optional climb to the monastery is the day's highlight if you have the legs.

stages.name: El Port de la Selva → Cap de Creus

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The finish: about 15km along the coast and over the wind-scoured rock of the Cap de Creus headland, past coves and the strange tramuntana-eroded stone to the lighthouse and the trail's end where the Pyrenees meet the Mediterranean. Easy grades but exposed and waterless — the only water is at the lighthouse bar, so carry your own.

objective.name: GR10
objective.summary: La Grande Traversée des Pyrénées follows the green French flank of the range coast to coast — Hendaye on the Atlantic to Banyuls-sur-Mer on the Mediterranean, village to village, waymarked red-and-white.

guide.heading: Distance, elevation & duration
guide.body: Roughly 926.6 km with about 55,086 m of cumulative ascent over 55 stages.

blocks.label: Moderate–Hard
blocks.note: Long days · up to 2,400 m ascent
June to September; lower and wetter than the Spanish side, with reliable village stages. Snow lingers on the high cols into early summer.

guide.heading: What to carry
guide.body: Most walkers run a 40-50 litre pack, since the gîte-and-refuge network means you carry food for the day rather than days of supplies. Bring sturdy lightweight boots with a good tread for boulderfields and wet ground, plus two poles, which the guidebooks strongly recommend on the long descents into the valleys. Even in August, high overnight stops can drop near freezing, so pack a fleece, a thermal layer & full rain gear; afternoon storms are routine, so waterproofs go in even on a clear morning. Three litres of carrying capacity covers the longest dry climbs, and in an early-season heavy-snow year mini-spikes can be useful on lingering névé near the high passes.

guide.heading: Following the blazes
guide.body: The GR10 is waymarked end to end with the red-and-white GR blaze — a white stripe above a red stripe — painted on rocks, posts & walls, with a cross marking a wrong turn and an angled flash signalling a change of direction. Marks cluster at forks and junctions, so in good visibility navigation is straightforward. The exception is fog on the high passes, where the painted marks thin out and a GPS track or map and compass earn their place. Phone coverage is reliable in the valley towns but goes intermittent above roughly 1,800 m, so don't rely on a live map alone.

guide.heading: Where to sleep
guide.body: The backbone of the GR10 is the gîte d'étape — a French walkers' hostel with dormitory beds, an evening meal & breakfast, and usually a packed lunch on request. Staffed mountain refuges fill the higher, remoter stretches on similar half-board terms (budget roughly 50-80 € a night), and the frequent village stages add hotels, pensions & chambres d'hôtes for a real bed. The infrastructure is dense enough to walk the whole route without a tent. Book ahead in July and August, and carry cash, as cards aren't always accepted at the mountain huts.

guide.heading: Filling up
guide.body: Water is one of the GR10's real advantages over the drier Spanish GR11: the wet, green French flank carries springs, streams & fountains for much of its length, and the route keeps dropping into valleys past villages where you can refill. Streams are generally bridged or easily forded in summer. The carries that matter are the long climbs to the high passes, where you leave the last water at the valley floor and don't meet more until the descent — fill to three litres before those. Treat water taken from streams below pastures, where grazing stock is upstream.

guide.heading: Staying safe
guide.body: The real hazard on the GR10 is rarely the terrain — it's a careless slip on an easy but exposed path, which is why two poles are strongly advised. Pyrenean weather turns fast: clear mornings build into afternoon thunderstorms, so cross the high passes early, ideally before 2 pm. The walking season runs mid-June to late September, when the snow has cleared the passes and the refuges are open; outside that window you need winter experience. In an emergency dial 112 for the PGHM mountain rescue, give your position, and remember that coverage is patchy above the valleys — staffed refuges are your reliable point of contact and shelter.

guide.heading: Belts of green, and who lives in them
guide.body: The GR10 climbs through stacked vegetation bands you can read by altitude: oak and mixed deciduous woodland low down, beech and silver fir through the montane belt, mountain pine (Pinus uncinata) on the damp north-facing slopes of the subalpine zone, then open alpine meadow above the treeline, which sits around 2,200–2,450 m. The flower meadows of Béarn and Bigorre carry asphodel, Pyrenean iris, columbine and wild orchids through early summer. For wildlife, the animal you'll likely see most days is the izard, the Pyrenean chamois, smaller and more agile than its Alpine cousin and grazing up to about 3,000 m; marmots whistle from sub-alpine pastures (reintroduced after dying out here post-glaciation). Overhead, griffon vultures are near-daily company, and you may pick out the 3 m wingspan of the bearded vulture (gypaète) in the central French Pyrenees.

guide.heading: Four cultures, west to east
guide.body: The GR10 reads as a slow handover between mountain peoples: Basque in the Pays Basque, Béarnais and Gascon through Aspe, Ossau and Bigorre, then Catalan as you drop toward Canigou and the sea. Euskara, the Basque language, has no relation to any other tongue in Europe, and you'll meet it on the first days as place names, the pelota fronton in every village square, and red-shuttered whitewashed farmhouses (etxe). The Couserans and Haute-Ariège are the trail at its emptiest — villages far apart, long climbs, fewer people — while the Catalan east mixes French and Spanish (and Catalan) in the same conversation. The cultural shift is as marked as the landscape's, and the GR10's village stages and gîtes d'étape put you inside it rather than camping above it.

guide.heading: Eat the valley you're in
guide.body: Because the GR10 strings together so many village stops, you eat regionally rather than from a stove. In the Basque and Béarn west, that means garbure — the thick cabbage, bean and ham-hock soup of the Pyrenean farmhouse — piperade (peppers, tomato, egg), poulet basquaise and axoa seasoned with Espelette pepper, and ttoro, a Basque fish stew nearer the coast. The mountain cheese to look for is Ossau-Iraty, a pressed ewe's-milk cheese named for the Ossau valley and the Iraty forest, PDO since 1996. For the trail or a café stop, gâteau basque is the standard: a dense pastry cake filled with either black-cherry jam or almond cream. Half-board (demi-pension) at a gîte d'étape is usually the easiest way to eat a regional dinner after a long stage.

guide.heading: A frontier walked by smugglers & escapers
guide.body: This border has always been porous, and the GR10's paths inherit that traffic. Local passeurs — Basque smugglers and mountain guides who knew every stream valley — ran contraband across the watershed for generations, and in WWII the same knowledge moved people. In the Basque west, Andrée de Jongh's Comet Line guided some 300 downed Allied airmen over low crossings near the Bidassoa toward San Sebastián, often by night. Further east in the Ariège, the Chemin de la Liberté out of Saint-Girons climbed high into Catalonia; thousands escaped over the Pyrenees between 1940 and 1944 — refugees, airmen, those fleeing forced labour and persecution — guided by safe-house keepers and couriers, many of whom paid with their lives. You're walking the gentler, waymarked version of routes that were once a matter of survival.

guide.heading: Wet in the west, dry in the east — & up before noon
guide.body: The GR10 walks the Atlantic-to-Mediterranean gradient end to end. The Basque start is one of the wettest corners of France — mist, drizzle and lush green — and the trail dries and brightens steadily as you move east, finishing in the warm, rockier, Mediterranean-influenced Catalan Pyrenees above Banyuls. The season is roughly late June to September, after the high passes shed their snow. The daily rhythm that matters most in July and August is the afternoon storm: hot, slightly unstable days build cloud over the ridges, and clear morning skies can turn to thunder at altitude within an hour. Start early, aim to be over the day's high col by early afternoon, and treat a building anvil cloud as the signal to get down.

sections.name: Pays Basque
sections.tagline: Green Atlantic foothills from Hendaye through the Basque country.
sections.summary: Green Atlantic foothills from Hendaye through the Basque country.

guide.heading: Grass-and-sandstone foothills, not yet the high range
guide.body: This stretch climbs over the steep, grassy, wooded hills of the Basque Country before the limestone arrives — rolling cols rather than alpine passes for the first days. La Rhune (905m) is the first real summit out of Hendaye, and the red sandstone Crête d'Iparla above Bidarray (Pic d'Iparla, 1044m) is the first true ridge: a few kilometres of exposed crest where the wind blows hard. Past Saint-Jean-Pied-de-Port the ground rises into the beech country around Iraty and the gorges of Holzarté and Kakuetta cut deep below the path.

guide.heading: Pasture, then the largest beech forest in Europe
guide.body: The early stages cross open grazing — "a place for grazing" is what Larrun/La Rhune means in Euskara — where semi-feral pottok ponies, a centuries-old Basque breed, range the slopes. East of Saint-Jean-Pied-de-Port the trail enters Iraty, the largest beech forest in Europe, straddling France and Spanish Navarre between the Okabe plateau and Pic d'Orhi over some 17,000 hectares. Around Saint-Étienne-de-Baïgorry the lower ground carries the terraced vines of Irouléguy, the only vineyard in the French Basque Country.

guide.heading: Pilgrim roads and single-street bastides
guide.body: Ainhoa is a bastide-rue, its whitewashed Labourdin houses lined along one avenue that pilgrims once walked toward Compostela by the Baztan route, and Sare beside it is listed among Les Plus Beaux Villages de France. The section ends — by the usual reckoning — at Saint-Jean-Pied-de-Port, the second busiest start of the Camino Francés, where tens of thousands of pilgrims set out each year beneath the old citadel. Saint-Étienne-de-Baïgorry gives its valley wine its name, and Sainte-Engrâce keeps an eleventh-century Romanesque church at the eastern edge.

callouts.body: The Basque country is notorious for thick, persistent fog — hikers leaving Kaskoleta have reported visibility down to 20 m — which can make the ridge and pass sections a hard, disorienting navigation slog.

The Crête d'Iparla above Bidarray runs along vertiginous sandstone cliffs and steep grassy slopes and should only be attempted in dry, clear weather; this stage is so demanding (about 1465 m of ascent) that many walkers skip it by transfer.

The final approach into the high Iraty/Cize country involves proper mountain days with long, steep ascents and descents, including the rugged Col d'Espalza where a fixed handrail aids a very steep passage that is awkward with a heavy pack or in bad weather.

sections.name: Haut-Béarn
sections.tagline: The Aspe and Ossau valleys into the first high country.
sections.summary: The Aspe and Ossau valleys into the first high country.

guide.heading: Pines on the limestone, pasture in the cols
guide.body: On the karst above Lescun the trees that hold on are pins à crochet, the hook pines, growing curved and stunted out of the rock. Lower and in the cols the ground turns to summer pasture grazed by free-ranging flocks, with the Braca d'Azuns beechwood shading the approach to the Camplong organs. Much of the eastern half lies inside the Pyrenees National Park, where the protected grasslands carry alpine flowers through the short high season.

guide.heading: Shepherds, cheese, and a navy's masts
guide.body: This is living pastoral country — cayolars (shepherds' cabins), free-roaming herds, and the ewe's-milk Ossau-Iraty made on both the Aspe and Ossau sides. Two pieces of built history bracket the stretch: the 11th-century Romanesque church at Sainte-Engrâce, founded by the abbey of Leire in 1085 as a pilgrim hospital on the Santiago road, and the Chemin de la Mâture above Etsaut, a ledge cut into the cliff in 1772 to drag fir trunks down for the masts of Louis XIV's navy.

guide.heading: Snow holds late on the high pass
guide.body: The Atlantic end of the range catches a lot of weather, and the high cols hold snow well into summer — the Hourquette d'Arre is commonly blocked until mid-June, sometimes to the month's end. The limestone plateaux are exposed with little shelter and few reliable streams once you leave the valleys, so the karst sections are best crossed in clear, settled conditions. Check the pass before committing to the Gabas–Gourette day early in the season.

callouts.body: The Chemin de la Mâture out of Etsaut is a path cut into a sheer cliff over 200 m above the Gave d'Aspe, narrowing to about 1.8 m in places above the Gorges de l'Enfer — wet rock, a heavy pack, or vertigo make this the most exposed passage of the whole GR10.

Across the karst plateau of La Pierre Saint-Martin (a mineral world of limestone between roughly 1,500 and 2,100 m), fog can roll in within minutes and there are few landmarks, so carry the IGN map, compass or GPS and don't rely on waymarks alone in poor visibility.

sections.name: Bigorre
sections.tagline: Cauterets, Gavarnie and the high Hautes-Pyrénées.
sections.summary: Cauterets, Gavarnie and the high Hautes-Pyrénées.

guide.heading: Three big cols & the roof of the GR10
guide.body: This is where the GR10 climbs highest: the Hourquette d'Ossoue at 2,734m, between the Oulettes de Gaube and Gavarnie, is the trail's top point, reached over loose rock with the Vignemale glacier alongside. Either side of it the days swing hard up and down — Col d'Ilhéou, Col de Madamète at 2,509m — separated by valley floors at Cauterets, Luz-Saint-Sauveur and Barèges around 700–1,250m. Expect granite, scree near the passes, and long descents on stony paths rather than gentle gradients.

guide.heading: High passes, afternoon sun & a short season
guide.body: These are the highest days on the GR10, so the season is short — paths around the Hourquette d'Ossoue and Col de Madamète hold snow into early summer, and Petit Vignemale needs ice axe and crampons before the July melt. Afternoon sun on the long exposed descents is fierce; trip reports stress sun protection and an early start to clear the passes before cloud builds. Phone signal is patchy between refuges, so check the forecast in the valley towns.

callouts.body: The Hourquette d'Ossoue at 2,734 m is the highest pass on the entire GR10 and holds snow late into the season, so early-summer walkers can meet lingering névé on this exposed crossing and should check conditions before committing.
This Cauterets-Gavarnie corridor is one of the busiest, most iconic stretches of the Pyrenees, so its refuges and gîtes fill early in July and August and should be booked well ahead.

sections.name: Luchonnais
sections.tagline: The Louron and Larboust valleys around Luchon.
sections.summary: The Louron and Larboust valleys around Luchon.

guide.heading: From beech valleys to summer pasture
guide.body: The walking shifts through bands as you climb: beech and fir woodland on the lower valley slopes around Astau and above Fos, then open grassland and summer pasture on the ridges toward Bacanère. The high ground here is grazed estive — grassy enough that the climb to Pic de Bacanère is mostly meadow rather than scree. Higher still, around the Araing lake, the cover thins to rock and short alpine turf.

guide.heading: A June-to-October window
guide.body: This is high, exposed terrain — the practical season runs June to October, with snow lingering on the cols into early summer. The border ridge to Pic de Bacanère is open and offers no shelter, so it is the place a passing storm matters most; the Araing approach is an avalanche path and not a winter route. Carry layers for the high cols even on a settled day, when valley warmth gives no sense of conditions 1,000m above.

callouts.body: The Fos-to-Araing stage climbs roughly 1,700 m to the Col d'Auéran (2,176 m) with an exposed ridge descent, where one hiker reported visibility dropping sharply at the col — high ground to avoid in storms or low cloud.

This is a long, demanding stretch with two big days of 1,200 m and 1,700 m of climb and long balcony traverses through wild, infrastructure-light country east of Luchon, so plan resupply and pace carefully.
Expect to meet patou guardian dogs working flocks on narrow cliff paths near Araing — give the herd a wide, calm berth rather than pushing through.

sections.name: Couserans
sections.tagline: The remote western Ariège — Biros, Bethmale and Bassiès.
sections.summary: The remote western Ariège — Biros, Bethmale and Bassiès.

guide.heading: Beech below, rhododendron above
guide.body: The walking moves through beech forest on the lower climbs and descents, opening into summer pasture and shepherds' huts higher up. Above the treeline the slopes carry rhododendron and bilberry, as on the approach to the col d'Escots, before the final beech woods drop you into Aulus-les-Bains. The pattern repeats stage after stage: forest, then estives, then rock.

guide.heading: A century of mining, then quiet
guide.body: The Biros valley was mining country for a century: lead and zinc ore came down from the high workings of Bentaillou and Chichoué to the bocard ore-mill at Eylie, which ran until the 1950s. The GR10 drops past this legacy into Eylie-d'en-Haut. Over the whole stretch stands Mont Valier (2,838 m), the emblematic summit of the Couserans, named for the mythical first bishop of Couserans said to have climbed it.

guide.heading: High cols, short days of cover
guide.body: The high crossings — port d'Aula, col d'Escots, col de Fitté — are exposed and hold snow late into spring, so the cabane d'Aula stages suit summer and early autumn. Water can be scarce on the long climbs: on the Saint-Lizier-d'Ustou to Aulus day the cirque de Casiérens is noted as the first reliable source since setting out. Aulus-les-Bains itself sits low at about 750 m, a warm valley floor after the tops.

callouts.body: After Eylie-d'en-Haut the trail enters a long section with no villages, crossing four successive ridges (Col de l'Arech 1,802 m, Clot du Lac 1,826 m, Col de Laziès 1,845 m, Col d'Auédole 1,727 m), so plan supplies and water for a committing, remote day.

The high passes around the Pic de Crabère and Col d'Auéran are exposed and snow-prone; the Étang d'Araing approach is strongly advised against in winter due to avalanche paths, and early-season snow can linger on these cols.

sections.name: Haute-Ariège
sections.tagline: The high eastern Ariège to the Carlit and Bouillouses.
sections.summary: The high eastern Ariège to the Carlit and Bouillouses.

guide.heading: From granite cirques to the high Carlit lakes
guide.body: This stretch climbs out of the Vicdessos valley and stays high, crossing passes over 2,300 m — the Porteille des Bésines at 2,333 m, the Col de la Coume d'Aniel at 2,487 m. The early stages drop into and out of the granite cirque of the Étangs de Bassiès, where staggered lakes sit on grey granite slabs; the later ones reach the Carlit massif and the dammed Lac des Bouillouses at roughly 2,020 m. The walking shifts from steep valley sides above Marc and Siguer to broad, lake-studded high ground around the Bésines and the Bouillouses.

guide.heading: Alpenrose at the treeline, peat bogs above
guide.body: Around the treeline you walk through dense Rhododendron ferrugineum — the alpenrose — which forms low shrubland up to about 2,500 m and flowers deep pink in early summer. Above it, hooked (mountain) pine gives way to open alpine meadow and to the peatlands that ring the Bésines and Bouillouses lakes; the Catalan Pyrenees alone hold some 1,600 peat bogs. The Plateau de Beille and the Carlit lake country are both protected for this biodiversity.

guide.heading: Iron miners and the black Mérens horse
guide.body: The villages below the trail — Goulier, Siguer, Sem — sent miners to the Rancié iron mine in the Vicdessos until it closed in 1931, and most have since lost the bulk of their population. Lower down, Mérens-les-Vals gives its name to the black Mérens horse, bred here for mine haulage and mountain work; it fell to around 40 registered animals by the early 1970s before being revived. Each June hundreds of Mérens still go up to summer pasture near 1,500 m.

guide.heading: Start early, the passes are exposed
guide.body: The high cols here are exposed and afternoon thunderstorms are common through summer — storms often build around 16:00, when temperature can drop sharply, so start the climbs to the Porteille des Bésines and Col de la Coume d'Aniel early. September brings more stable, quieter days but colder nights and a real chance of fresh snow at altitude. Some passes hold persistent snow that can call for care even into summer.

callouts.body: The Rulhe-to-Bésines section is rated highly challenging and for experienced hikers only, crossing exposed cols above 2,300 m where afternoon storms and lingering snow on the porteilles are real risks early and late in the season.
This is high, remote border country: the Crête des Isards links a succession of climbs and descents over 2,000 m with no villages between refuges, so carry food and plan refuge stops carefully.

Lakes and streams are plentiful through the Bassiès cirque and the high étangs near the Bésines, and the manned refuges (Bassiès, Rulhe, Bésines) are open and staffed through the summer hiking season.

sections.name: Catalan Pyrenees
sections.tagline: Cerdagne, Canigou and the descent to the Mediterranean at Banyuls.
sections.summary: Cerdagne, Canigou and the descent to the Mediterranean at Banyuls.

guide.heading: From granite plateau to the sea
guide.body: This is the Pyrenees winding down: you start on the open Cerdagne–Capcir plateau above Planès, cross the Carança and Mantet reserves on cornice paths cut into steep cliff, then climb the Canigou massif — at 2,784 m the last great summit of the eastern chain. After Cortalets the trail loses height for good, dropping through the Vallespir to Arles-sur-Tech before a final ridge walk over the Albères to the Mediterranean at Banyuls.

guide.heading: Four bands in a week
guide.body: The Mantet reserve alone stacks four vegetation levels — collinéal, montane, subalpine and alpine — from valley meadow at 950 m to bare rock near 2,500 m. Walk east and the gradient repeats trailwide: spruce and beech around Mariailles and Cortalets give way below Batère to chestnut and oak, and the last stages run through cork oak, broom and Mediterranean scrub shaped by the wind.

guide.heading: Sun, heat, and the tramontane
guide.body: The high opening stages run mostly without shade, so the Carança and Canigou days carry full sun and need an early start. As the trail drops toward the coast the heat builds rather than eases — walkers regularly find the last days near the Mediterranean the hottest of the trek. The shaping force at the eastern end is the tramontane, the dry wind off the land that has bent the Albères vegetation and can hit the final ridge hard.

callouts.body: The summit pitch of Pic du Canigou via the "cheminée" chimney is a hands-on scramble that, as of 2023, is no longer waymarked — stay on the GR10 / Tour du Canigou path around the north flank unless you are an experienced scrambler in dry, settled conditions.

This is high, exposed terrain — the Col du Pal sits near 2,294 m and Canigou tops 2,784 m — so afternoon thunderstorms and lingering snow on the high cols are real risks; the reliable walking window here is roughly July to October.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The trail starts on Hendaye beach at the Atlantic and works inland over a string of low, rolling cols — Osin (374m), des Joncs, Ibardin — to the hamlet of Olhette. A long but gentle opening at around 26 km; the climbs are easy, so the distance is the thing to pace.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The steepest pull comes right at the start, up toward La Rhune (905m), the first real Pyrenean summit and a Basque sacred mountain, before dropping through Sare to the bastide of Ainhoa. Get the climb done early — after La Rhune it eases into village walking.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: Rolling hills and open pasture lead from Ainhoa to Bidarray, sat on the Nive, with a couple of real ascents along the way. A transitional day before the ridge — the village is the launch point for Iparla the next morning.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The first true mountain day: a long climb onto the red sandstone Crête d'Iparla (Pic d'Iparla, 1044m), a few exposed kilometres of crest where griffon vultures wheel, then down to Saint-Étienne-de-Baïgorry. Walk it only in dry weather — wind on the ridge and a cave passage make it no place for rain or cloud.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A shorter, easier link out of the Irouléguy wine valley to Saint-Jean-Pied-de-Port, the old pilgrim town and gateway to the Camino Francés. A recovery-paced day after Iparla; the citadel town is the main resupply and rest stop of the section.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: Leave Saint-Jean-Pied-de-Port and climb steadily up the Nive valley, past Estérençuby near the river's source, to the Gîte d'étape Kaskoleta. The ground starts rising in earnest here — this is the approach to the high beech country, so save legs for what follows.

stages.name: Gîte d'étape Kaskoleta → Gîte d'étape Chalets d'Iraty

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A sustained, demanding climb up to the Chalets d'Iraty, entering Iraty — the largest beech forest in Europe — straddling the Spanish border high on the range. The big elevation gain is the day's work; the reward is the forest and the open ridges above it.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From the Iraty chalets the route runs over the high ground near Pic des Escaliers (1472m) and along ridges before the long descent to Logibar in its valley. A high, exposed day with a steep drop at the end — mind the knees on the way down.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From Logibar the path climbs past the Holzarté suspension footbridge, slung 180m above the Olhadübi gorge, then traverses high shepherd country above the canyons to Sainte-Engrâce. The bridge and the gorge edges are the day's defining ground — spectacular, and exposed enough to watch your footing.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A climbing day out of the Basque valley of Sainte-Engrâce — pause at its 11th-century Romanesque church — up onto the limestone toward the ski station at La Pierre Saint-Martin, on the edge of the great Larra karst. The walk is steady ascent on grazing slopes; the thing to watch is the shift onto bare, exposed limestone with little water near the top.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The crossing of the Arres de Camplong, a wide limestone plateau of lapiaz and hook pines, with the pyramid of Pic d'Anie (2504m) ahead before a long drop into the Lescun cirque. Footing on the karst is slow and the descent to Lescun loses around 1200m, so it walks longer than the distance suggests.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A quieter transition stage leaving the Ansabère cirque behind and dropping through pasture and woodland into the depths of the Aspe valley at Etsaut. Mostly descent and forest — a recovery day between the limestone above and the big climb that follows.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The signature day of the section: the Chemin de la Mâture, a path hewn into the cliff of the Gorge de l'Enfer, then a long climb to the Ayous lakes where Lac Gentau mirrors the Pic du Midi d'Ossau (2884m), before descending to Gabas. It is a big sporting stage with around 1490m of ascent — the exposed Mâture ledge is the part to take slowly, especially when wet.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The high point of the whole GR10: a sustained climb from Gabas to the Hourquette d'Arre at 2465m, then down past Lac d'Anglas to the ski resort of Gourette. The one thing that matters is the pass itself — it holds snow into late June, so confirm it's clear before setting out early in the season.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A high-pasture day over the Col de Tortes (1799m), Col de Saucède and Col du Soulor, leaving Béarn for the Val d'Azun and Bigorre at Arrens-Marsous. Gentler than the days before, with shepherds' cabins and griffon vultures riding the escarpments — the long final descent is the main effort.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long, steady climb out of Arrens-Marsous over the Col de Bordères and past the Lac d'Estaing, then up the Ilhéou valley to the Col d'Ilhéou before dropping to the lake and refuge — around 1,600m of ascent. It is one of the harder days on this stretch, so pace the early kilometres rather than burning out before the final col.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A short, mostly downhill morning following the Gave d'Ilhéou past the Lac Noir and through the Cambasque valley into Cauterets. Easy walking and an early finish — use the spare time to resupply and rest in town before the big Vignemale days ahead.

stages.name: Cauterets → Refuge des Oulètes de Gaube

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From Cauterets the trail climbs through forest beside the gave to Pont d'Espagne, then up past the Lac de Gaube to the Refuge des Oulettes de Gaube under the north face of the Vignemale. It is a sustained ascent on a very popular path; the reward is camping beneath the highest peak in the French Pyrenees, so leave time to watch the glacier light up.

stages.name: Refuge des Oulètes de Gaube → Chalet-refuge la Grange de Holle

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The high point of the whole GR10: a rocky, loose climb to the Hourquette d'Ossoue at 2,734m, then a long, undulating descent with stream crossings and waterfalls toward Gavarnie and the Chalet-refuge la Grange de Holle. It is a big day of ups and downs — what matters most is clearing the pass early, as the descent runs hot and exposed in the afternoon.

stages.name: Chalet-refuge la Grange de Holle → Luz-Saint-Sauveur

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A descending day off the high country, dropping from the Gavarnie valley down to Luz-Saint-Sauveur at around 700m. Knees take the strain on the long downhill, but the reward is a full-service valley town to resupply and clean up before the Néouvielle leg.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A shorter valley-to-valley hop, climbing out of Luz-Saint-Sauveur on a balcony path above the Bastan to reach Barèges, the highest thermal station in France. Straightforward walking that sets you up for the climb into the Néouvielle reserve — a good half-day if you want an afternoon at the baths.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The walk into the Néouvielle Nature Reserve: up the Aygues-Cluses valley past the Madamète lakes to the Col de Madamète at 2,509m, then down past the Aumar and Aubert lakes on a balcony path to Lac de l'Oule. The granite-and-lakes scenery is the highlight of the section, so allow time — and respect the reserve's protections (no fires, stay on path).

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From Lac de l'Oule the trail climbs to the Col de Portet at around 2,215m, then crosses the Saint-Lary ski-area pastures with a wide panorama over the Aure valley before descending to Vielle-Aure. A long downhill finish off the high lakes — pace the descent and enjoy the last open views before the valley closes in.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A valley-to-village stage out of Vielle-Aure in the Aure valley, climbing away from the National Park gateway over pasture and through hamlets toward Germ at around 1,340m. Steady ascent rather than high drama — a transition day that sets up the lake country ahead.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A big mountain day from Germ over high cols into the Astau valley and up to the Oô cirque, ending at one of the Pyrenees' best-known lakes. Lac d'Oô is fed by a cascade of roughly 275m, so save energy and water for the long climbs on either side of it.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From Lac d'Oô up past the Espingo cirque, then a high traverse beneath Pic du Céciré and along the ridges by Superbagnères before dropping into Bagnères-de-Luchon. The defining feature is the descent: a very long, knee-heavy drop to the valley, so pace the final hours into town.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A shorter climbing stage leaving the spa town through Juzet-de-Luchon and Sode up to the hamlet of Artigue at around 1,230m near the frontier. Treat it as the build-up to the border ridge — Luchon is the place to resupply before the high crossing tomorrow.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The high point of this section: nearly 1,000m of grassy ridge-climbing to Pic de Bacanère at 2,193m, walking the France–Spain border past frontier stones with the Maladeta and Aneto on the skyline. A long descent through woodland follows to the small village of Fos, with little shelter on the exposed ridge if weather moves in.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long sustained climb of around 15km from Fos up through cols to the refuge at the Étang d'Araing (1,965m), beneath the Pic de Crabère. This is the gateway into Ariège and the wilder Couserans — the refuge is open roughly June to September, so confirm before relying on it at the season's edges.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A short stage that gives back almost everything you climbed earlier: from the étang d'Araing refuge it drops over a thousand metres past the old Biros mining workings into Eylie-d'en-Haut. The descent is the day — long and knee-heavy — so save energy for it, or add the Pic de l'Har variant if you want the height to count.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The first proper Couserans climbing day out of Eylie, across beech forest, summer pastures and shepherds' huts toward Ayer. Wild and quiet country — pace the long ascent, because shade and water thin out above the treeline.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A valley-and-pasture stage into the small farming hamlet of Esbintz, where the gîte sits right on the trail. The main thing here is logistics: Esbintz is one of the gîtes that lends sleeping bags and gear for the unstaffed cabane d'Aula, so sort your kit before pushing on.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A climb into high granite country to the cabane d'Aula, set in a cirque under the Cuns d'Aula. The cabane is unstaffed, so carry what you need — bedding and food — and don't count on finding either up here.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The high crossing of the stretch: past the étang d'Areau and over the port d'Aula, then a long drop toward Couflens and up to the hamlet of Rouze. The port is the crux — exposed and snow-prone into early summer, so check conditions before committing.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A short, easy descent off Rouze into the green Ustou valley to Saint-Lizier-d'Ustou. After the cabane d'Aula days it's a recovery leg — a chance to resupply and sleep under a roof.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long, full day over the col de Fitté and the col d'Escots, marking the passage from the Couserans into the Haute-Ariège, finishing in the old thermal town of Aulus-les-Bains. Water is the catch: the cirque de Casiérens is the first reliable source on the route, so fill up before the climb.

stages.name: Aulus-les-Bains → Refuge des Étangs de Bassiès

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A climb out of Aulus back into high lake country, ending at the refuge above the étangs de Bassiès. Straightforward mountain walking, but it's a real ascent off the valley floor — settle into the rhythm of the climb early.

stages.name: Refuge des Étangs de Bassiès → Marc

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From the Bassiès refuge the GR10 leaves the granite cirque and its staggered lakes, heading southeast on a stony trail before a long drop toward Marc — a stage with real descent on tired legs, so save something for the knees.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A valley-and-climb stage up out of the Vicdessos toward the old mining village of Goulier, which sits above 1,000 m. The day is steady walking through former miners' country rather than high passes.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A shorter linking stage across the upper Vicdessos to Siguer, another village that supplied the Rancié iron mine. It is transitional walking — useful for resupply and rest before the climb onto the Plateau de Beille.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The long pull up onto the Plateau de Beille, a broad pastoral plateau between 1,650 and 2,000 m that doubles as a cross-country ski domain. Expect sustained climbing with little shade — water and pace matter on the ascent.

stages.name: Plateau de Beille → Refuge du Rulhe

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: About 19 km of high ridge walking from the plateau over a string of cols — Finestres, Didorte, Belh — to the Refuge du Rulhe, with roughly 1,100 m of climbing and clear views throughout. It is a big, exposed day: start early to stay ahead of afternoon storms.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From the Rulhe the trail crosses the Col de la Calmette, skirts the Étang Bleu and follows a ridge before a long descent down the Mourguillou to Mérens-les-Vals. The drop into the valley is sustained — manage the descent rather than rushing it.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From Mérens the high mountains open up: a long climb along the Nabre torrent to the Porteille des Bésines at 2,333 m, then down to the guarded Refuge des Bésines by its lake. This is exposed high ground, so time the pass for the morning.

stages.name: Refuge des Bésines → Refuge des Bouillouses

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A high crossing through the Carlit lake country, over the Col de la Coume d'Aniel at 2,487 m past the Lac de Lanoux — the largest lake in the French Pyrenees — to the dammed Lac des Bouillouses near 2,020 m. The most important thing is the altitude: the cols are over 2,400 m and weather turns fast up here.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The final stage of this stretch leaves the Cerdagne plateau of Bouillouses and descends through pine forest toward the meadows around Planès — a smoother, mid-altitude walk after the high lakes. It is the gentlest day of the section, a wind-down off the Carlit.

stages.name: Planès → Refuge du Ras de la Carança

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long, sun-exposed day off the Cerdagne plateau, dropping from Planès into forest and valley before the steady climb to the Ras de la Carança refuge — around 1,170 m of ascent with almost no shade, so fill up and start early.

stages.name: Refuge du Ras de la Carança → Mantet

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A shorter, friendlier stage into the Mantet reserve, reaching the hamlet of Mantet and its welcoming gîte. The reserve crossing is the point of the day: a quiet alpine valley on the Spanish border, climbing toward the cols above the village.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The trail rounds the Canigou massif's western flank to the Refuge de Mariailles, with scree underfoot on the higher sections. It's a transitional day — you leave the Carança–Mantet country and set up for the big climb tomorrow.

stages.name: Refuge de Mariailles → Refuge des Cortalets

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The climb onto Canigou: from Mariailles the GR10 works up to the Cortalets refuge at 2,150 m, the launch point for the 2,784 m summit. The summit is an out-and-back variant, not the GR10 line — decide before you go whether you're taking it.

stages.name: Refuge des Cortalets → Refuge de Batère

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From Cortalets the trail traces the Canigou balcony with long views to Roussillon and the sea, then descends to the Batère refuge, set in former iron-mine buildings. This is where the high mountains end and the trail begins losing height for good.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A descent day into the Vallespir, dropping through chestnut and oak woods to Arles-sur-Tech. The valley heat and the long downhill are what to manage — easy navigation, hard on the knees.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: Leaving Arles-sur-Tech, the GR10 climbs back out of the Tech valley and works through the wooded foothills toward Las Illas, close to the Spanish border. Lower, hotter, and more enclosed than the high country — carry water for the climbs.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A forested approach to the Albères, ending at the Col de l'Ouillat below Pic Néoulous — the last night before the sea. Steady walking through cork oak and beech; the col is the staging point for the final coastal descent.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: The finish: over Pic Néoulous and the Albères ridge, past Pic Sailfort's view over Banyuls and the Bay of Roses, then down through vineyards and dry-stone huts to the Mediterranean. Long, exposed and often the hottest day of the trek — pace the descent to the beach at Banyuls-sur-Mer.

objective.name: Aneto
objective.summary: At 3,404 m the highest summit in the Pyrenees — three ways up, from a glacier walk to a technical ridge.

guide.heading: The mountain
guide.body: A big glaciated massif; the easiest line is long and high rather than hard, but the summit stride is exposed.

guide.heading: When to go
guide.body: July to September, once the glacier is walkable and before autumn snow.

guide.heading: Conditions
guide.body: Glacier crevassing worsens through summer; the Paso de Mahoma is unforgiving in wind.

routes.name: Vía Normal · La Renclusa
routes.tagline: The glacier route over the Paso de Mahoma — physically demanding rather than technical.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: From the Renclusa hut you cross the Aneto glacier to the Portillón, then tackle the airy Paso de Mahoma onto the summit. An alpine start, crampons, and a rope for the glacier.

callouts.body: Crevassed glacier — rope, ice axe and crampons; a guide if unsure.
Paso de Mahoma is exposed — turn back in wind.
Storms build on summer afternoons — summit early.

routes.name: Cara Norte
routes.tagline: A steeper line on the north face — real ice, a serious day.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A steeper, more committing line up the north face — real ice and a serious day out.

routes.name: Cresta de Salenques
routes.tagline: A long technical ridge traverse onto the summit — for the experienced.

blocks.heading: Overview
blocks.body: A long, technical ridge traverse from the Coronas valley onto the summit — for the experienced only.
